message: poisonland
The old place got too crowded and we got told to move somewhere else.

Pass here! Pass here!

Haha, the ball's mine!
Get out of the way, kid. You're ruining our game!
Ah! You gave me a fright, I thought I was hallucinating. I didn't think I'd see another soul again.

replies.text: The Doughe was worried you had already perished. Good thing you found this safe spot.

message: I applied a special reagent at the entrance that kept the monsters away, but I used it all up. Good thing the Doughe sent you. I was about to run out of supplies too.

replies.text: How come you didn’t just leave and stayed stuck here for so long?

message: I was about to ask you, $playername, how come you managed to enter this accursed cavern in the first place...
You and Eckhart look at each other dumbfounded. A moment later, you begin to hear a loud rumble from the direction you entered this cave from, along with vibrations throughout the entire cavern.
This is what I was referring to. The same thing happened to me. When I tried leaving, the passage to the exit was blocked by a spill of lava I thought was impassable.
I assumed you had just found a different way, but it seems like we’re both stuck here now.
I don't know. Go and check what happened. Maybe I’m wrong, and this time it's different.
The pool of lava sits blocking the passage, emanating intense heat.

replies.text: Seems that Eckhart's worries became true.

message: I came here to find Eckhart, I can't just ignore him and explore.
Have you checked if the rumble locked us in, or is the passage still open?

replies.text: It is a long story, but in short the way now is open. [Tell Eckhart about the monster and the fire spirits.]
No luck, the path is blocked by lava.

message: I had hoped we wouldn't have to resort to messing with the thing in the eastern cavern...

To our right is another cave, I couldn't just idle around for days while I was stuck, so I tried finding another way out. But deeper in there is a horrific creature, its massive deformed body hunches to fit in the cave. Past it is some sort of staircase. I was too scared to investigate, but right now that seems to be our only hope of leaving this place alive.
You did...? I'm impressed, where do those stairs lead?

replies.text: I found some fire creatures, spirits or whatever. By how sudden and unnatural the rumble was, it's quite obviously their doing
They lead up into a cave and outside. But it's up on the mountain, and I didn't see a way down.
I didn't explore further for an exit... I'll go now.

message: I'm amazed you could figure everything out on your own!
Have you had any success finding us an exit?

replies.text: I found some fire creatures, spirits or whatever. By how sudden and unnatural the rumble was, it's quite obviously their doing
I explored the mountain, but I couldn't find a way down.

message: You have to find something $playername, after all neither of us can climb down a sheer mountain.
And if you're telling me this, then you couldn't find a way to deal with them... Couldn't you simply destroy them like how you killed the monster?

replies.text: I tried to, but they would just reappear and mock me in our tongue, saying things like "gems" and "'pay." I know I'm as precious as a gem, but they're the ones who need to pay...

message: Did you manage to deal with the fire spirits?

replies.text: I tried to, but they would just reappear and mock me in our tongue, saying things like "gems" and "pay." I know I'm as precious as gem, but they're the ones who need to pay...
After enough gems, one of the human-shaped fire spirits spoke to me. They found the offerings fitting and said their arrangement here is over, and they will stop the lava.
I didn't try kill them yet.

message: More like a glass gem you mean, still I don't blame you $playername. I think I understand what they meant.

Spirits and the like, especially those who follow orders, require adequate payment. I don't know how long they've been here, but our only chance is to give them enough of what they're asking for to outdo the original payment, or what remains of it, considering they must have been here for ages.

Glass gems and rubies are near worthless, you would need too many of them for the spirits to consider them of value, and polished sapphires are only slightly better. Sharp amethyst and sparkling jade would make suitable offerings. The most perfect of all would be Oegyth crystals, but those are the stuff of legend, perhaps it was with one of those that the spirits were bound for hundreds of years.
We're free to go then. Good job $playername.

After being stuck here for days, thinking my end was near, when I saw you it felt like you were a savior sent to help me by Elythara herself. I have a purpose to fulfill, which is why no one but me volunteered for this job. Now with a clear path forward I'm convinced of it.

With the spirits gone, the way in should be open too. I cannot stop and leave, $playername. If I flee, the corruption won't heal and the spread of disease won't stop. This whole ordeal would have been for naught. Please take my notes and deliver them to the Doughe. We both have our own missions to fulfill, after all.

Eckhart adjusts his gear to fit snugly, then turns to you and puts his hand on your shoulder. He gives you a smile, then walks off to the other side of the cave until he passes the corner and his quiet footsteps fade away.

The fire spirits you thought you had just cut, reform back into their original form from mere sparks. Originating above what looks to be an altar, all the while speaking words you can surprisingly understand...
Crackle… crackle…
[Crrck.] Gems...[Crackle.] Pay...
The creature before you is made entirely of flame, whirling as the wind blows.
The creature before you is made entirely of flame, yet unlike the other two, its form is stable, resembling more a person engulfed by fire than a natural phenomenon.
Crrckk... Sizzle...
Crrckk... Sputter...
The fire spirits originated from this altar. Perhaps the word “gem” it said has something to do with it.
The glass gem burns with a weak flame...

replies.text: So I must simply burn gems.

message: The glass gem burns with a weak flame...
As the gem burns on the altar, the crackling behind you intensifies, and a sizzling sound follows as the fire person speaks.
The ruby gem burns with a flame...
The polished gem burns with a medium flame...
The sharpened gem burns with a large flame...
The sparkling gem burns with a great flame...
That's not something you need to know about. Now leave.
Enough... Your offerings were sufficient.
Millennia ago, we agreed to be bound here and serve for a price, but your offerings exceeded what remained of the original. The lava will soon dissipate, and we are free to roam once more.
The gem burns. The flame great enough to envelop the whole altar... Once it subsides, the gem remains intact.
The crackling behind you intensifies, and a sizzling sound follows as the fire person speaks.

The Kazaul offered us enough energy to dwell beneath the sun at the price of mere lava. But the crystals worth has diminished, making this form unstable and unpleasant. You have paid off the remaining decades of our toil, the lava will soon dissipate, and we are free to roam once more.
The fire that stood before you dissipates in a mere moment, as if it were never there. Only traces of heat remain in the air.

replies.text: Huh, I guess that did it.

message: And what are you trying to achieve by coming here and bothering me?

replies.text: I just don't like the feeling of being lied to by that Feygard professor. I want to know what really happened.
It might be interesting to hear it.

message: Interesting? It was a mistake to tell my nephew anything about the past. I won't repeat it, especially not to a strange kid like you.

replies.text: Can you just answer my question?
It's interesting because it might be an adventure for me to solve.

message: Knowing the truth wont give you better grades. Nothing good comes from digging this up.

replies.text: Can you just answer my question?
That wasn't my motivation from the beginning. I don't attend the academy, I'm traveling around in search of my brother Andor.

message: To solve you say...?
The last time I was in Brightport, I heard rumors of some commotion in my hometown of Charwood. They said a young adventurer descended into the mines and slew a dragon called 'Thukuzun.'

The last time I was in Brightport, I heard rumors of some commotion in my hometown of Charwood. Creatures called goblins emerged from the mines one day and overran the town. Some people did not manage to flee in time and were captured, but a brave young adventurer saved them.
You must be out of your mind, kid. I'm certain you're up to no good here. Leave.
...Could that have been you?

replies.text: Indeed, I was the one who helped Maevalia and the people of Charwood.
In flesh and bone, unlike Thukuzun, who was nothing but bone.

message: You start to get on my nerves now...
In search of your brother? Hah, You really think that I'd believe that...?
If you are who you claim to be, bringing me the Charwood sap should prove no difficult task.

replies.text: Here is a bottle of Charwood sap.

message: *Sigh* it fell in the river. We're about to head home.

replies.text: Here's 5 gold. Get a new one.

message: You're that kid from before.

replies.text: I see your ball is missing.

message: Travelling...? I'm starting to feel crazy believing the words of a child like you. Even so, that look in your eyes is convincing me you're not here to joke around...
By the shadow, what has assailed me? Living here alone with nothing but my thoughts must've affected me worse than I thought.
I know what to do... Bring me a bottle of Charwood sap, then I'll talk...

replies.text: I actually have one with me. [Give the sap]

message: Awesome, thanks!
The kids get up and leave.
You haven't left with you friends?

replies.text: They're not my friends. I don't even know their names.

message: Hi kid, I would still advise you to turn back. Beyond here lies only a dead end.

replies.text: Can you tell me about Charwood again?
Why are you guarding a dead end then?

message: I'd still advise you to turn back, this way you're leaving Brimhaven and further in is a dead end.

replies.text: Why are you guarding a dead end then?

message: The man grabs the bottle and takes a big gulp from it.
Ah, that hit a spot. Thank you, kid. I should introduce myself first. My name is Heiter.
It is the Battle of Brightport you wanted to know? Take a seat.

I suppose I shall start from the very beginning... I was born in Charwood. We were three brothers in our family. My dad was a miner, like many in Charwood, but none of us wanted that kind of life for ourselves.

The eldest of us went to Nor City, but me and my older brother Heinkel stayed. Once I came of age, I moved to neighboring Brimhaven and began work as a forest ranger, but Heinkel stayed in Charwood, working for the Oakburn family who were the wealthiest in Charwood.
The monsters aren't so kind as to care for boundaries like we do. I don't know what's happened up in Charwood, but quite a few monsters have come my way recently. Nothing I can't handle.

replies.text: I don't know where that is, could you explain?
Charwood? The way ahead goes to Charwood?

message: Brimhaven and Charwood were much closely related up until recently. It was someone from Brimhaven came across a few ore deposits in the hills a few hundred years ago and decided to open a mine.
Bit strange not to have heard of Charwood, eh? What do they even teach you kids these days?

The family of that individual founded the settlement and became extremely wealthy. Once they had Feygard's backing, they wanted more and reached the bottom of the mine but... [Leans in and whispers] Feygard wanted a bigger slice of the pie too, and the family began relentlessly exploiting the miners to dig deeper. A few years ago, the miners finally had enough and kicked them out of Charwood, ruling the settlement by themselves. That's when the passage got cut off.

Since the family ran away to Nor City, Feygard just accepted that and started dealing with the townfolk directly. I heard they hit a new vein too. Good for them. But that is what you get for dealing with those treacherous Feygardians. You grow up into a fine Brimhavener, kid.

replies.text: I'm not from here but sure.

message: Err, forget everything I just said then. You haven't heard a word from me.
Since the family ran away to Nor City, Feygard just accepted that and started dealing with the townfolk directly. I heard they hit a new vein too. Good for them.

Soon the Noble Wars began. Brimhaven stood with Nor City, but the Oakburns refused to follow. Eventually they joined, but the other wealthy families held a grudge against them for it. When the troops of Nor City arrived, I worked for them as a scout, keeping note of enemy positions in the area and reporting any movements. I came close to being gravely hurt many times, but thanks to the Shadow I came out relatively unscathed through those years.
As you might have heard at the academy, those rich folk who held all the influence in our town quickly switched sides when Feygard was at our doorstep. So did my patron. And so did Heinkel's.

They feared that Feygard would punish them once the war was over, so they conceded to every demand in the hope it would bring them into Feygard's good graces. Food, even as we hungered, labor, and our meager troops as auxiliaries. Charwood had little to spare save for iron from its mine, which was beginning to deplete. So the Oakburns sent men from their household's employ, among them my brother. I myself then had to work as a scout for the Feygardians.

As I was heading west with a group of Feygard soldiers, the main force was preparing to march east to take Brightport from the Eastern Coalition, my brother among them. I had faith the Shadow would watch over him, as it had over me in those years.

When I returned it was a horrific sight. Much of the main square had been turned into a makeshift hospital. Rows were filled with Feygard knights I had only ever seen as mighty and prideful men. Some were missing limbs, others were beyond healing. Not even bonemeal potions could save them. I searched frantically for my brother, but I couldn't find him anywhere

The men from Brimhaven who were sent alongside them, stood as auxiliaries in the back rows, and were left relatively unscathed. Save for those in the Oakburn's employ... The other families conspired with a commander and made arrangements so that the men from Charwood were sent to the front lines, lightly armored as they were.

I asked everyone who was in a state well enough to speak. I found one man who was fresh recruit and marched east alongside my brother. When the Feygardians were caught in an ambush, Heinkel was sent as reinforcements to the front lines instead of that young man's battalion.

He fought bravely, as I knew my brother would. But as the battle began to stabilize, something horrific happened. A horde of horrific creatures assailed the battlefield. It wasn’t an attack by Nor City, no sane person would believe that. They were something far worse than that. Those creatures targeted the weaker ones first, and Nor City lost even more than Feygard. But my brother... he was torn apart by a disfigured giant. I couldn’t believe it when I heard it.

West of this cabin built on land I was given for my service to Feygard, was the battleground. I didn't witness it firsthand. My brother did, and he lost his life there... I only heard about it from those who were there.

A fate as horrible as this, for my brother... The Shadow had forsaken us in those needless wars. And now I wonder. Did my brother die with any regrets? Since then I've witnessed the land west of here be poisoned by this curse before my very own eyes.
Heiter puts the bottle of Charwood sap back on the table. Before you noticed, he had already drunk the whole bottle.

Sorry $playername, that was a lot to put on you so suddenly. The Doughe and I have been monitoring this situation for the past few years, but it has never been this bad before. If you wish to assist directly, you should go and talk with Rubiano. Tell him Heiter sent you.

replies.text: Thank you for trusting me with this.
Yeah... that was a lot. Bye.

message: Good to see you in good health once again, $playername.

replies.text: Can you tell me your story again?
I was looking for help in getting into that cave just west of here.

message: Some things are better left unrepeated, I hope you'll understand that.
That is the old Laeroth reliquary. It's where Korhald, the founder of Remgard, is entombed.

replies.text: Do you know how I can get inside?

message: No I don’t, but it’s better you stay away from it. Disturbing the dead is never wise...
